deck: Four early Dimagi employees most involved in building CommCare join Jonathan Jackson to retrace the 14-year journey from bespoke projects to a low-code platform, and the design under the mango tree philosophy that grounded it all in real users.
seo_description: Four early Dimagi builders on the making of CommCare: designing under the mango tree, learning from users, and the shift from custom projects to a product.
og_description: The making of CommCare: designing under the mango tree and the shift from custom projects to a product.
about: In part one of a five-part series marking Dimagi's 20th anniversary, Jonathan Jackson sits down with four early employees who were most involved in creating CommCare, the low-code platform that lets NGOs and governments build applications for their frontline teams. Used by a million people across more than 130 countries, CommCare was built over 14 years through an unusual story of tenacity, humility, and continuous learning from users.

The conversation traces Dimagi's earliest wild west days of bespoke, custom-coded projects, the realization that every one of those projects created a long maintenance burden, and the gradual, often skeptical shift toward a repeatable product. Along the way the team unpacks the move from windows mobile PDAs to Nokia brick phones, the offline-first instincts forged by building software in remote settings, and the participatory design sessions held literally under a mango tree south of Dar es Salaam.

You will hear how multimedia features added so low-literacy users could use CommCare were unexpectedly turned by health workers into behavior change tools, how case management and tracking data over time set CommCare apart from peers, and how it eventually became a product that non-engineers could build on. Speaking are Dr. Neal Lesh, Dr. Brian DeRenzi, Cory Zue, and Clayton Sims, sharing the pivots, disagreements, and lessons from the making of CommCare.

pull_quote.text: We Dimagi calls it design under the mango tree, and there's actually a mango tree. We'd grab a bench and pull it out outside of the health center and sit literally under a mango tree and have these feedback sessions.
pull_quote.cite: Dr. Brian DeRenzi, on design under the mango tree

bullets: The wild west years: how Dimagi's early bespoke projects each became a long-tail maintenance burden that made a product approach feel necessary
Design under the mango tree: participatory design sessions held literally under a tree south of Dar es Salaam, with users driving the decisions
Feeling the pain of users: building offline-first because the team faced the same connectivity limits as the field, sharing code over a local repo
Underestimating users: the client who noticed a swapped phone and asked where his data went, a lasting lesson on privacy and sophistication
Multimedia's surprise: audio and images added for low-literacy workers became behavior change tools when health workers showed them to clients
From config files to a product: the long, nonlinear path from hand-built XML forms to an app builder anyone could use on CommCare HQ
Going deep to go wide: why tracking data over time and case management, hard problems most products would skip, became CommCare's secret sauce

span of the journey to create CommCare

guests.name: Dr. Neal Lesh
guests.role: Chief Strategy Officer, who helped lead the early work that became CommCare and coined the name in a proposal.

guests.name: Dr. Brian DeRenzi
guests.role: Global Director of Research Strategy, who did much of the early CommCare field work and ran the design sessions under the mango tree.

guests.name: Cory Zue
guests.role: Dimagi CTO from 2007 to 2017, an early skeptic of the product approach who built much of the server side that became CommCare HQ.

guests.name: Clayton Sims
guests.role: Dimagi's second and current CTO, who led much of the early mobile engineering and offline-first design of CommCare.
